_full_text:
Summary of Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA Plus +)
assessment
For the Passport Automated Decision-Making in IRCC’s Global
Case Management System (GCMS)
Overview
Automation in GCMS, as the single passport issuance system across Canada, was introduced in
December 2019 as part of the Passport Program Modernization Initiative (PPMIi) in response to
needs for improving integrity and system capabilities (i.e. improve screening and vetting
procedure) as well as to address within service standards the anticipated fluctuations in
passport volumes including the expected increase in 10-year passport application renewals.
This modernization effort includes a digital process designed to move applications through a
series of decision gates to
o establish if it can find the applicant’s identity in the legacy Integrated Retrieval
Information System (IRIS), Central Index (CI) or GCMS database;
o determine if the applicant is eligible and entitled to a Canadian travel document;
o indicate if a Passport Program employee needs to review the applicant’s information
that may impact their entitlement before issuance of the passport.
Unless GCMS identifies a reason requiring further review or verification, applications (mostly
adult renewals) submitted by known adult applicants for which there are no identity, eligibility,
or entitlement concerns will run seamlessly through the automation process and be auto-
approved. All other applications, such as those for children, unknown adult applicants, and any
client for which GCMS finds signs of identity, eligibility, or entitlement concerns will drop out of
the automation process to be reviewed by a Passport Program employee. In other words, the
automation process will focus the attention of Passport Program employees where it is most
needed.
Objective and Scope
The objective of this standalone GBA Plus+ analysis is to examine the impacts of Passport
automation on applicants since it was introduced in 2019. Based on identity criteria such as
gender, age and language of application, the Passport Program examines how any identified
unintended outcomes may be mitigated while supporting processing and particularly how
applicants are triaged by the system to streamline identity, eligibility, and entitlement
determinations.
Moving forward, the goal of the GBA Plus analysis is to create a baseline from which to assess
impacts of various subsets of passport applicant populations and the way they are processed in
GCMS. In addition, the analysis promotes improved system processes and functionalities, and
helps to establish a monitoring regime through on-going quality assurance evaluations to be
conducted on automation rules. In other words, this GBA Plus will ensure equitable and fair
treatment of all Canadian citizens, who qualify for Canadian regular passport services.
1
Limitations
This GBA Plus+ assessment of the Passport automation solution notes the following limitations:
Adding additional statistical data:
Due to privacy concerns and in accordance with direction from the Auditor General the Passport
Program has for many years not captured disaggregated data.
Due to limited capacity and functionalities not fully developed, including additional statistical
data for various subsets of passport applicant populations and the way they are processed in
GCMS proved to also be a challenge.
Data Sample:
The sample of post-automation applications is limited as the data was pulled from the pre-pilot
and pilot phases of the implementation. With the full GCMS deployment where more
applications will be leveraged through automation, the Program will be able to determine the
best way to apply GBA+ monitoring through a better data sample representation.
Metrics to Explain Differential Impacts:
Using data from rates of approval and non-approval to determine if the introduction of
automation changes the outcome of application processing can provide an overview of the
impacts of automation. When it comes to breaking down the proportion of applicants in
assigned categories it shows that there may be differences that may fast-track some and stall
other applications; for example non-renewals (children and adult applications) drop out
respectively at 100% and 98% for manual review needing more time to be reviewed.
Concerning the criteria on ‘language’, the GBA Plus Analysis is based on an applicant’s choice of
language in the application form submitted, therefore no differentiation is made based on this
element. For example, the challenge is to determine if Francophone applicants are being
impacted by processing delays.
Methodologyii
The sample collected for the purposes of this GBA plus assessment contains more than 91,000
post-automation applications processed by Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC)
and IRCC since automation was introduced in 2019.
Initially, the statistical data compared:
 the proportion of applications, before it was run through automation
 the proportion of applications triaged into low complex applications and;
 the high complexity files that are transferred to a passport officer for manual review.
2
This determines as to whether approval and non-approval rates change with the introduction of
Passport automated processing and decision-making.
The analysis then identifies any variations or differential outcomes based on age, gender, and
language used during the application process to determine any bias or other unintended
impacts. As per GBA Plus requirements, any +/-4% variations are noted and discrepancies are
further explained. It is imperative that the data continues to be reviewed/validated by Passport
Officers (POs) and run through the on-going monitoring process.
Results
Data analysis from the pre-pilot and pilot stages indicate that automation does not appear to
have any significant, disproportionate impacts on passport population (i.e. those applying
according to Adult Renewal, Adult and Child application types). In particular, automation does
not change the criteria used by passport officers to determine who is entitled to passport
services.
The noted discrepancies in triaging files based on full automation, which are low complexity
adult renewals (35% of the inventory) vs those partially automated which are high complexity
non-renewals (65% of inventory, including adult and children) are due to the larger percentage
that are dropped out of automation for manual processing by virtue of being non-renewals and
thus out of scope for full automation. This also ensures applications for vulnerable population,
such as children are afforded a level of careful review in order to address any potential adverse
impacts or safety concerns from complexities due to diverse family structures including custody,
foster care, adoption and surrogacy.
The gender profile has remained largely unchanged both when triaged for full automation and
dropping out for manual review by the officer and other processing levels. There were a few
exceptions to this pattern. For instance, there is a higher percentage of female applicants
(53.37%) vs men (46.63%) when triaged to full automation. 49.10% of child passports were
issued to females, compared to 50.88% for males.
The distribution of applications by age is done through a Program decision on Passport
automation, according to the three application types:
 Adult renewals and adult applications: group aged 16 and above;
 Children applications: group of ages 0-15.
This age categorization is also applicable to the automated passport processing and decision-
making. No significant and disproportionate impacts on end decisions have been identified
based on age within the adult renewals and children, first time applicants and other adults.
When triaged for manual review these applications result in an approved final decision and
accordingly issued Full Validity Passports (FVP) and Limited Validity Passports (LVP).
Also, the ratio of using English and French as the language for applying has been retained as a
result of triaging in full automation vs those partially automated. It has been proportionate to
the applications received, processed and issued in GCMS, too. Yet, for applicants submitting an
application in French, the approval and non-approval rates have been respectively 89.86% and
10.14%, which varies (above the 4% threshold ) compared to those in pre-automation period.
3
However, due to significant differences in total volumes (over 44 million and 91K respectively
for pre- and post-automation) it is difficult to explain the variance.
The Program is committed to establishing a monitoring plan to review the automation business
rules, including drop-out criteria, these differences will be studied further to recommend
mitigations to any unintended outcomes. In terms of a future iteration, the Program is also
considering collecting, tracking and reporting on differential impacts in terms of processing
times between automated and human decisions. Actions to enable monitoring, reporting and
assess impacts of policies are currently underway. Modernization initiatives to change to a new
passport issuance platform should enable the Passport Program to better reporting and data-
collection to provide further insights to inform any disproportionate impacts on clients through
the expansion of the program.
i
PPMI is a multi-year project focused on replacing the current domestic passport issuance system with a new platform which
includes a front-end intake tool, Tempo, and automated Global Case Management System (GCMSi) at the back end for passport
processing. The four main objectives are:
o Leverage IRCC’s system that chronologically structures client information.
o Enhance the efficiency, integrity and security of passport processing.
o Focus examination activities on higher-risk and higher-complexity applications.
o Maintain Canadians’ access to passport services including service standard commitments within revenues with shorter
turnaround times for Canadians even in periods of high demand.
ii Methodology is based on the assumption that Passport automation rules derive from Passport Officer (PO) application processing
rules while the automated system will continue to be tested for unintended biases and, once fully deployed, will be monitored for
quality assurance to protect against unintended outcomes.
4
